title: Cakey brownies
subtitle: Lighter brownies with a drier, more open crumb
excerpt: Cakey brownies have more flour and a small amount of baking powder, which gives them a more open crumb and a drier texture than the fudgy style. They are baked until a skewer comes out clean — no wobble, no underbaking. The result is closer to a very dense chocolate cake.
sourceNotes: The cakey brownie is the earlier American form, appearing in Fannie Farmer's Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and Sears, Roebuck and Company recipe collections of the early twentieth century, before the fudgy style became the modern preference. Both forms remain in domestic cookbooks throughout the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut and wrap individually. Freeze for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: These brownies are good the day they are baked and remain good for 3 days in an airtight tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: The two main brownie textures are controlled primarily by the flour-to-fat ratio and the degree of baking. Fudgy brownies have less flour and are pulled from the oven while the centre is still molten; cakey brownies have more flour and are baked until a skewer comes out clean. Both styles use the same base ingredients — the differences are in proportion and doneness, not in method.

content.text: is a matter of flour ratio and doneness. This version uses twice the flour of the fudgy recipe and a little baking powder, and is baked all the way through. The result has a drier, more portable texture — better for packed lunches or bake sales than the fudgy style.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Melt {{dark-chocolate}} and {{unsalted-butter}} together and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Whisk {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together in a large bowl until combined — 1 minute, no need to beat to ribbons for the cakey style. Stir in the melted chocolate mixture. Fold in {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{salt-table}} until just combined.

Pour into the tin and bake for 25–28 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean with no raw batter clinging to it. The top should be set and the sides pulling away slightly from the tin. Leave in the tin to cool for at least 30 minutes before cutting. For neat squares, refrigerate for 1 hour then cut with a sharp knife.
